title: Tempered dark chocolate
subtitle: Tabling-method temper for couverture
excerpt: Tempered dark chocolate by the classical tabling method: melt at 50°C, work two-thirds on a cold surface down to 28 to 29°C, return to the bowl to raise the temperature to 31 to 32°C. Use for moulding, dipping, and decorations. Five hundred grams.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier and earlier nineteenth-century French confectioners document the tabling method (le tablage) for tempering couverture. Pellaprat, L'Art Culinaire Moderne (pre-1928, public domain in French): documents the temperature windows for dark, milk and white chocolate.

content.text: Tempering chocolate means melting it, then cooling it through a set temperature curve so the cocoa butter sets in the stable Form V crystal. The result is chocolate that snaps cleanly, looks glossy, sets at room temperature, and does not bloom white over time. The tabling method works two-thirds of the melted chocolate on a cold marble surface to seed the Form V crystals. That cooled chocolate then goes back into the warm chocolate to raise the temperature into the working window.

Use couverture chocolate with at least 30% cocoa butter, not eating chocolate. The cocoa butter content is what controls the temper. A digital probe thermometer is essential. One degree between in-temper and out-of-temper matters.

items.name: Dark chocolate
items.prepNote: couverture, 70% cocoa, finely chopped or callets

content.text: Set a wide saucepan with 3 cm of water on low heat. Set a metal bowl over the pan, base just above the water. Add the chopped {{dark-chocolate}} and stir gently with a spatula as it melts. Check the temperature with the probe. Stop when the chocolate reaches 50°C and is fully melted; lift the bowl from the heat.
Table two-thirds, down to 28 to 29°C, see the

Pour two-thirds of the melted chocolate onto a cool marble surface. Use a palette knife and metal scraper to work the chocolate continuously, spread it thin, scrape it back together, push it out again. Keep working for 4 to 5 minutes; the chocolate will thicken visibly as it cools. Check the temperature; stop at 28 to 29°C. The chocolate should hold a peak briefly when lifted on the palette knife.

Scrape the cooled chocolate back into the bowl with the warm reserved third. Stir gently to combine; the temperature should settle around 31 to 32°C. If too cool, set the bowl over the warm water for 5 to 10 seconds at a time, stirring, checking the temperature. Test the temper: dip the tip of a spatula and set on a piece of baking paper. It should set hard and glossy in 3 minutes at cool room temperature, with a clean snap when broken. If it stays soft or sets dull, the temper failed, re-melt and re-table.
